question_text: When debentures are issued at a premium, the premium is credited to:
explanation: When debentures are issued at a price higher than face value, the excess (premium) is credited to Securities Premium Reserve Account. This is the same account used for share premium. Entry: Bank Dr. (total), To Debentures A/c (face value), To Securities Premium Reserve A/c (premium).
subject: accountancy

options.text: Capital Reserve Account
Securities Premium Reserve Account
General Reserve Account
Debentures Account directly

elimination_hints.hint: Capital Reserve arises from capital profits (e.g., forfeiture). Debenture premium goes to Securities Premium.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing capital reserve with securities premium.

elimination_hints.hint: General Reserve is from revenue profits. Debenture premium is a specific reserve under Section 52.
Debentures A/c records only the face value. Premium goes to a separate reserve account.

question_text: When debentures of ₹100 each are issued at ₹95 (i.e., at a discount of ₹5), the discount is:
explanation: Discount on issue of debentures is a capital loss. It is debited to 'Discount on Issue of Debentures' Account (shown under 'Other Non-Current Assets' as an unamortized expense). It is written off over the life of the debentures through the Statement of Profit & Loss.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Credited to Securities Premium
Ignored — no accounting treatment needed
Debited to 'Discount on Issue of Debentures' and written off over the debenture term
Debited to Debentures Account directly

elimination_hints.hint: Securities Premium is for amounts ABOVE face value. Discount is BELOW face value — it's a loss, not a gain.
Discount must be accounted for — it's a real cost of borrowing that needs to be recognized.
Debentures A/c always reflects face value. Discount is recorded in a separate account.

question_text: A company issued 10,000, 9% debentures of ₹100 each at ₹105. The entry is:
explanation: Issue at premium: Bank receives ₹105 × 10,000 = ₹10,50,000. Debentures A/c is credited at face value = ₹10,00,000. Premium = ₹5 × 10,000 = ₹50,000 to Securities Premium. Entry: Bank Dr. 10,50,000, To 9% Debentures A/c 10,00,000, To Securities Premium Reserve 50,000.
subtopic: Entry for debenture issue at premium
subject: accountancy

options.text: Bank Dr. ₹10,50,000 → To 9% Debentures ₹10,00,000, To Securities Premium ₹50,000
Bank Dr. ₹10,00,000 → To 9% Debentures ₹10,00,000
Bank Dr. ₹10,50,000 → To 9% Debentures ₹10,50,000
Bank Dr. ₹10,00,000, Discount Dr. ₹50,000 → To 9% Debentures ₹10,50,000

elimination_hints.hint: ₹10,00,000 ignores the premium of ₹50,000. Total cash received is ₹10,50,000.
Debentures A/c must be at face value (₹10,00,000). Premium goes to a separate reserve.
This is a discount entry, but the issue is at premium. No discount exists here.

question_text: A company issued 8,000, 10% debentures of ₹100 each at ₹90. The entry is:
explanation: Issue at discount: Bank receives ₹90 × 8,000 = ₹7,20,000. Debentures A/c credited at face value = ₹8,00,000. Discount = ₹10 × 8,000 = ₹80,000. Entry: Bank Dr. 7,20,000, Discount on Issue of Debentures Dr. 80,000, To 10% Debentures A/c 8,00,000.
subtopic: Entry for debenture issue at discount
subject: accountancy

options.text: Bank Dr. ₹8,00,000 → To 10% Debentures ₹8,00,000
Bank Dr. ₹7,20,000 → To 10% Debentures ₹7,20,000
Bank Dr. ₹7,20,000, Loss on Issue Dr. ₹80,000 → To 10% Debentures ₹8,00,000
Bank Dr. ₹7,20,000, Discount on Issue Dr. ₹80,000 → To 10% Debentures ₹8,00,000

elimination_hints.hint: ₹8,00,000 cash was NOT received. Only ₹7,20,000 was received (at ₹90 each).
elimination_hints.misconception: Showing face value as cash received.

elimination_hints.hint: Debentures must be at face value (₹8,00,000), not the discounted amount.
The correct account name is 'Discount on Issue of Debentures', not 'Loss on Issue'.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using wrong account name (though concept is similar).

question_text: Discount on issue of debentures is written off:
explanation: Discount on issue of debentures should be written off over the LIFE (tenure) of the debentures. Each year, a proportionate amount is charged to the Statement of Profit & Loss. Entry: Statement of P&L Dr., To Discount on Issue of Debentures A/c. It can also be written off from Securities Premium if available.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Entirely in the year of issue
Over the life/tenure of the debentures proportionately
Only at the time of redemption
Never — it remains as an asset permanently

elimination_hints.hint: Writing off entirely in one year would distort that year's profits. Matching principle requires spreading over the tenure.
Waiting until redemption would overstate assets during the debenture life. Gradual write-off is the correct approach.
Discount is a wasting asset — it must be written off. It cannot stay permanently.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating a temporary item as permanent.

question_text: When debentures are issued as collateral security for a bank loan, the Balance Sheet treatment is:
explanation: Debentures issued as collateral security are shown as a NOTE below the Balance Sheet (or as a footnote). They are NOT shown as a liability because the primary liability is the bank loan itself. No journal entry is passed — only a memorandum entry.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Shown as a separate liability under Non-Current Liabilities
Added to the bank loan amount
Disclosed by way of a note — not shown as a separate liability
Shown under Current Liabilities

elimination_hints.hint: Showing as separate liability would double-count the borrowing. The bank loan is the primary liability.
Adding to the bank loan would overstate the total borrowing. They're the same debt, not separate.
Even if shown, debentures are long-term — never under current liabilities. But they're not shown at all as a liability.

question_text: A company issues debentures for purchase of machinery worth ₹5,00,000. 4,500 debentures of ₹100 each issued at a premium of ₹10. The premium on debenture issue is:
explanation:
Number of debentures = 4,500. Premium = ₹10 per debenture. Total premium = 4,500 × ₹10 = ₹45,000. Entry: Vendor Dr. 5,00,000, To Debentures 4,50,000, To Securities Premium 45,000. Wait — face value of 4,500 debs = 4,50,000. Premium = 45,000. Total = 4,95,000 ≠ 5,00,000. Let me reconsider.

Actually: Vendor owes ₹5,00,000. Debentures issued = 4,500 × ₹110 (face + premium) = ₹4,95,000. Hmm, that's a ₹5,000 shortfall. The question might mean the total issue = face value × debentures + premium covers ₹5,00,000. With 4,500 @ ₹100 = ₹4,50,000 face + ₹45,000 premium = ₹4,95,000. The question simply asks for the premium amount = ₹45,000.
subtopic: Premium on debenture issue to vendor
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹50,000 = 5,000 × 10. But only 4,500 debentures are issued, not 5,000.
₹5,00,000 is the machinery value, not the premium. Premium = 4,500 × ₹10.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using total asset value as premium.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹4,50,000 is the face value of all debentures. Premium is the additional ₹10 per debenture.

question_text: The entry for recording debenture interest due (gross amount ₹50,000, TDS 10%) is:
explanation: When debenture interest becomes due: Debenture Interest A/c Dr. ₹50,000 (gross expense), To Debenture Holders A/c ₹45,000 (net payable), To TDS Payable A/c ₹5,000 (tax deducted at source). The company deducts TDS before paying interest.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Debenture Interest Dr. ₹50,000 → To Bank ₹50,000
Debenture Interest Dr. ₹45,000 → To Debenture Holders ₹45,000
Debenture Interest Dr. ₹50,000 → To Debenture Holders ₹50,000
Debenture Interest Dr. ₹50,000 → To Debenture Holders ₹45,000, To TDS Payable ₹5,000

elimination_hints.hint: This bypasses Debenture Holders A/c and ignores TDS. The accrual and payment are separate entries.
Expense must be at GROSS amount (₹50,000). The company's expense doesn't reduce because of TDS.
This ignores TDS. The company must deduct tax before paying, creating a TDS liability.

question_text: A company issued 6,000, 12% debentures of ₹100 each at a discount of 5%, redeemable at a premium of 10%. The total loss on issue of debentures is:
explanation: Discount on issue = 5% × 100 = ₹5 per debenture. Premium on redemption = 10% × 100 = ₹10 per debenture. Total loss per debenture = ₹5 + ₹10 = ₹15. Total loss = 6,000 × ₹15 = ₹90,000. This total loss is the 'Loss on Issue' that must be written off.
subtopic: Combined loss — discount on issue + premium on redemption
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹30,000 = 6,000 × ₹5 = only the issue discount. The redemption premium (₹60,000) must also be included.
₹60,000 = 6,000 × ₹10 = only the redemption premium. The issue discount (₹30,000) must also be included.

question_text: The entry when debentures are issued at a discount and redeemable at a premium is:
explanation: Entry: Bank Dr. (cash received = face − discount), Loss on Issue of Debentures Dr. (discount + premium on redemption = total loss), To Debentures A/c (face value), To Premium on Redemption of Debentures A/c (redemption premium liability).
subtopic: Entry for discount issue + premium redemption
subject: accountancy

options.text: Bank Dr., Loss on Issue Dr. → To Debentures, To Premium on Redemption
Bank Dr. → To Debentures only
Bank Dr., Discount Dr. → To Debentures (no premium on redemption recorded)
Bank Dr. → To Debentures, To Securities Premium

elimination_hints.hint: This ignores both the discount and the premium on redemption. Both must be recorded.
Premium on redemption must be recorded as a liability at the time of issue itself — it's a known future obligation.
Securities Premium is for amounts received ABOVE face value. Here the company is PAYING a premium, not receiving one.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing premium received with premium payable.

question_text: Debentures can be issued at a discount, unlike shares.
explanation: TRUE. Under the Companies Act, 2013, shares cannot be issued at a discount (Section 53), but debentures CAN be issued at a discount. The discount must be properly accounted for and written off over the debenture's life.
subject: accountancy

options.text: True
False

elimination_hints.hint: Section 53 prohibits share discount, but debenture discount is permitted. Different rules for different instruments.

question_text: Premium on redemption of debentures is recorded as a liability at the time of issue itself.
explanation: TRUE. When debentures are issued with a premium on redemption, the premium payable on redemption is a known liability from day one. It is recorded as 'Premium on Redemption of Debentures' (liability) at the time of issue and debited to 'Loss on Issue of Debentures'.
subject: accountancy

options.text: True
False

elimination_hints.hint: Under conservatism principle, a known future liability should be recognized immediately, not deferred to redemption date.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking liability is recorded only at redemption.

question_text: When debentures are issued at a price below their face value, the difference is called _______ on issue of debentures.
explanation: The difference between face value and issue price (when issue price < face value) is called 'Discount on Issue of Debentures'. It is a capital loss that must be written off over the debenture's life.
subject: accountancy

question_text: TDS on debenture interest is deducted at the rate of _______ % under the Income Tax Act.
explanation: Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) on debenture interest is deducted at the rate of 10% under the Income Tax Act. The company deducts TDS before paying interest to debenture holders and deposits it with the government.
subject: accountancy

question_text:
Assertion (A): Loss on Issue of Debentures is written off over the life of the debentures, not in one year.
Reason (R): The matching principle requires that expenses be matched with the revenue/benefit period.

Choose the correct option:
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. The loss on issue (discount + redemption premium) is a cost of borrowing that benefits the company over the entire tenure of the debentures. The matching principle demands spreading this cost across the benefit period.
subtopic: Matching principle for loss on issue
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: Matching principle is EXACTLY why the write-off is spread. It directly explains A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not seeing matching principle as the reason.

elimination_hints.hint: The matching principle is a fundamental accounting concept. R is correct.
A is correct — gradual write-off over the debenture life is the standard treatment.

question_text: A company issued 10,000, 10% debentures of ₹100 each at a discount of 6% on 1st April 2025. Debentures are redeemable after 5 years. The amount of discount written off each year is:
explanation: Total discount = 10,000 × ₹6 = ₹60,000. Debenture life = 5 years. Annual write-off = ₹60,000 / 5 = ₹12,000 per year.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹6,000 = 6% of ₹1,00,000 (face value of 1,000 debentures). Total discount is on 10,000 debs = ₹60,000.
₹60,000 is the total discount for all 5 years. Per year = ₹60,000/5 = ₹12,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Writing off entire discount in one year.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹10,000 = ₹60,000/6. There are 5 years, not 6.

question_text: Debenture interest is debited to:
explanation: Debenture interest is an expense — it is debited to the Statement of Profit & Loss (or Profit & Loss Account). It reduces the profit of the company. It is NOT an appropriation — it is charged before arriving at net profit.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Statement of Profit & Loss (as an expense/finance cost)
Balance Sheet (under Reserves)
Appropriation section of P&L
Capital Reserve Account

elimination_hints.hint: Interest is an expense, not a reserve. It goes to P&L, not the Balance Sheet.
Appropriation is for dividends and reserves. Interest is a charge BEFORE profit, not from profit.
Capital Reserve is for capital profits. Interest is a recurring business expense.

question_text:
Match the debenture issue scenario with the correct journal entry:

| Column I (Scenario) | Column II (Entry — Debit side) |
|---|---|
| (i) Issued at par | (p) Bank Dr. + Loss on Issue Dr. |
| (ii) Issued at premium | (q) Bank Dr. |
| (iii) Issued at discount | (r) Bank Dr. + Discount Dr. |
| (iv) Issued at discount, redeemable at premium | (s) Bank Dr. |

Choose the correct matching:
explanation:
(i) At par → Bank Dr. only → (q) or (s) [same]
(ii) At premium → Bank Dr. (more than face) → (s) [Bank Dr. only — premium goes to credit side]
(iii) At discount → Bank Dr. + Discount Dr. → (r)
(iv) At discount, redeemable at premium → Bank Dr. + Loss on Issue Dr. → (p)

Correct: (i)→(q), (ii)→(s), (iii)→(r), (iv)→(p)
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Issue at par has no loss — just Bank Dr. (q), not Bank + Loss (p).
elimination_hints.misconception: Adding loss to a par issue.

elimination_hints.hint: Premium issue has Bank Dr. only (s). Discount is (r). Don't swap them.
Discount + redemption premium has both losses (p), not just discount (r).
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring redemption premium in combined scenario.

question_text: Loss on Issue of Debentures can be written off against:
explanation: Loss on Issue of Debentures can be written off against: (1) Securities Premium Reserve (if available), or (2) Statement of Profit & Loss over the tenure of debentures. Securities Premium can be used for this purpose under Section 52 of the Companies Act.
subtopic: Sources for writing off loss on issue
subject: accountancy

options.text: Only Statement of Profit & Loss
Only Securities Premium Reserve
Only Capital Reserve
Securities Premium Reserve or Statement of Profit & Loss

elimination_hints.hint: P&L is one source, but Securities Premium can also be used (Section 52 permits it).
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing Securities Premium can be used.

elimination_hints.hint: Securities Premium is one option. If insufficient, P&L is the fallback.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing P&L is also an option.

elimination_hints.hint: Capital Reserve is not typically used for this purpose. Securities Premium and P&L are the correct sources.

question_text: When debenture interest is paid, the entry is:
explanation: When debenture interest is paid (after TDS deduction): Debenture Holders A/c Dr. (settling the liability), To Bank A/c (net payment to holders), To TDS Payable A/c has already been recorded at accrual. If combined: Debenture Holders Dr., To Bank (net amount paid to holders).
subject: accountancy

options.text: Bank A/c Dr. → To Debenture Holders A/c
Debenture Holders A/c Dr. → To Bank A/c
Debenture Interest A/c Dr. → To Bank A/c
Debentures A/c Dr. → To Bank A/c

elimination_hints.hint: This shows cash coming IN. But interest payment means cash goes OUT — Bank should be credited.
Interest A/c was debited at accrual (when due). Payment settles Debenture Holders, not Interest again.
Debentures A/c is for the principal (face value). Interest has its own accounts.
